coredata.dc:title: Non-toxic Cs3Bi2Br9 quantum dots molecular imprinted nanocomposite with boronate affinity and imprinting selectivity for selective detection of oxytetracycline
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Xiao
Gao, Yue
Zhang, Shen
Chen, Aixia
Liu, Jiali
Wang, Fei
Kerboua, Ilhem
Yan, Mengwei
Wang, Jiaqi
Zhang, Yuhui
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a non-toxic bismuth-based perovskite quantum dot fluorescent nanocomposite (Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs) was constructed for the detection of trace oxytetracycline (OTC) in the ethanol phase, which solves the toxicity problem of perovskite quantum dots and realises the application of non-toxic perovskite quantum dots in analysis and detection, the combination boronate affinity effect and molecularly imprinted technology enables highly selective detection of the Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs. The basic structure and optical properties of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs were characterized by various characterization methods, and the optimal detection conditions and fluorescence detection ability of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs for OTC were determined. Under the optimal conditions, the Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs had a good linear relationship with OTC in the concentration range of 0.8 ∼ 130 μM, and the detection limit was 0.32 μM. Furthermore, the fluorescence detection mechanism of the Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs was investigated in detail, which ultimately revealed that the detection mechanism was a synergistic effect of inner filter effect and fluorescence resonance energy transfer. The Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs was successfully applied to the detection of trace OTC in real samples. This work provides a new idea for the construction of environmentally friendly and highly selective perovskite quantum dot fluorescent nanocomposite.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bismuth-based perovskite quantum dots
Boronate affinity
Molecular imprinting
Fluorescence detection
Oxytetracycline

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and modification of Cs3Bi2Br9 PQDs
Determination of OTC in soil samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number 42207101, 42220104005); the Key Research and Development Program of Shaanxi Province (grant number 2022ZDLSF06-06); and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, CHD (grant number 300102293208).

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number 42207101, 42220104005); the Key Research and Development Program of Shaanxi Province (grant number 2022ZDLSF06\u201306); and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, CHD (grant number 300102293208).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs synthesis process.
TEM images of (a) Cs3Bi2Br9 PQDs, (b) and (c) Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs.
The FT-IR spectrum of the Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs.
Fluorescence intensity and quenching ratio of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs (a) different TEOS dosages and (b) different APTES dosages.

(a) Absorbance of OTC in detection solution under different number of elutions. (b) Effect of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs concentration on detection system, and (c) Fluorescence intensity of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs solution at different pH conditions.
(a) Stability of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs and Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-NIPs in ethanol solvents and (b) Response time of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs for detecting OTC.

(a) Linear fitting curve between fluorescence quenching ratio (F0/F) of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs and the concentration of OTC. (b) Fluorescence spectra of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs with different concentrations of OTC (0 ∼ 130 μM). (c) Linear fitting curve between fluorescence quenching ratio (F0/F) of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-NIPs and the concentration of OTC and (d) Fluorescence spectra of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-NIPs with different concentrations of OTC (0 ∼ 70 μM).

(a) The relative quenching amounts of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs against OTC (20 μM) and other tetracycline antibiotics (20 μM). (b) Anti-interference study of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs against OTC (20 μM) in coexistence with other tetracycline antibiotics (20 μM).

(a) UV-absorption spectrum of OTC, the emission fluorescence spectrum of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs. (b) Suppressed efficiency of observed and corrected fluorescence of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs after addition of different concentrations of OTC and (c) Fluorescence lifetime attenuation curve of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs before and after OTC addition.
Response time and LOD of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs and relevant fluorescence nanocomposite for OTC detection.
The absorbance of OTC at the excitation and emission wavelengths of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs.
Fitting parameters of the PL decay curves.
Recovery of OTC in the spiked soil samples at different concentrations (n=5).

head.ce:title: Non-toxic Cs3Bi2Br9 quantum dots molecular imprinted nanocomposite with boronate affinity and imprinting selectivity for selective detection of oxytetracycline

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Subsurface Hydrology and Ecological Effects in Arid Region of the Ministry of Education, Chang’an University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a non-toxic bismuth-based perovskite quantum dot fluorescent nanocomposite (Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs) was constructed for the detection of trace oxytetracycline (OTC) in the ethanol phase, which solves the toxicity problem of perovskite quantum dots and realises the application of non-toxic perovskite quantum dots in analysis and detection, the combination boronate affinity effect and molecularly imprinted technology enables highly selective detection of the Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs. The basic structure and optical properties of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs were characterized by various characterization methods, and the optimal detection conditions and fluorescence detection ability of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs for OTC were determined. Under the optimal conditions, the Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs had a good linear relationship with OTC in the concentration range of 0.8 ∼ 130 μM, and the detection limit was 0.32 μM. Furthermore, the fluorescence detection mechanism of the Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs was investigated in detail, which ultimately revealed that the detection mechanism was a synergistic effect of inner filter effect and fluorescence resonance energy transfer. The Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs was successfully applied to the detection of trace OTC in real samples. This work provides a new idea for the construction of environmentally friendly and highly selective perovskite quantum dot fluorescent nanocomposite.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxytetracycline (OTC), one of the tetracycline antibiotics, is a broad-spectrum antimicrobial compound, that is widely used in the healthcare industry, animal husbandry, and other agricultural activities due to its advantages of low cost, rapid bacterial inhibition, and few side effects [1]. Currently, the unregulated use of OTC as a broad-spectrum antibiotic in production and breeding is becoming increasingly common. OTC enters the environment through multiple pathways and remains in environmental media such as water, soil, and plants, posing a threat to human health through the food chain [2–4]. The existing methods for quantitative detection of OTC include high-performance liquid chromatography [5], electrochemical methods [6], and immunoassay [7]. However, these methods are limited in the application of OTC detection due to the defects of high detection cost, complex operation, and long time consumption. Therefore, it is necessary to establish a simple and rapid analysis method with high sensitivity and selectivity to detect trace OTC in the environment.

Quantum dots (QDs) are semiconductor nanomaterials with unique properties compared with common fluorescent materials, the advantages of high stability, continuous absorption spectrum, and long fluorescence lifetime make them an ideal fluorescent material [8]. At present, fluorescence sensors based on QDs have been widely concerned because of their low cost, good stability and high sensitivity. Fluorescent sensors with QDs as fluorophores are widely used in complex analytical fields [9–11]. In recent years, quantum dot fluorescence sensors have been greatly developed, but there are still some problems such as low quantum yield, complex preparation and reduced sensitivity after modification. Fortunately, as an emerging fluorescent material, perovskite quantum dots (PQDs) make up for the shortcomings of traditional quantum dots to a certain extent due to their excellent optical properties, such as high quantum yield, strong light absorption, and tunable emission wavelength, which make them have a great potential for application in the field of fluorescence detection [12]. However, PQDs are not widely used in the field of analytical detection due to their high sensitivity to polar solvents [13], and the toxicity of lead also limits the practical application of PQDs. At present, most of the detection methods based PQDs are carried out by lead-based PQDs in non-polar solvents. Wu et al. [14] developed a simple and effective fluorescent sensor based on CsPbBr3 quantum dots for the detection of Sudan I-IV in hexane solvent. Tan et al. [15] prepared a highly luminescent CsPbBr3 quantum dot for pesticide detection. In practical applications, the instability of PQDs in polar solvents and the toxicity of lead element are still problems that need to be solved. Our group previously achieved the detection of tetracycline in the highly polar solvent ethanol for the first time by using a CsPbBr3 quantum dot fluorescent sensor [16]. Zhou et al. [17] prepared a new type of lead-free zero-dimensional indium-based perovskite Cs2InBr5-H2O, which could be used as a fluorescent sensor for the detection of trace water in organic solvents. These studies laid the foundation for the development of lead-free PQDs and their application in the ethanol phase. Our study was inspired by these works, bismuth is a "green metal" with many similarities to lead, so bismuth can be considered a substitute for lead to solve toxicity problems. The use of bismuth to replace lead is of great significance in solving the toxicity problem of PQDs and realizing the detection work in polar solvents.

Due to the defects on the surface of the QDs, it is easy to be interfered by coexisting substances in the detection process, which leads to the reduction of the selectivity of the fluorescence sensor. So suitable methods are needed to address the low selectivity of QDs fluorescent sensors. In complex environments, specific recognition can enable a specific selection of targets. Therefore, the modification of the surface of QDs to make them have the function of specific recognition can achieve highly selective detection of the sensor. Because of the unique boronic acid groups, the boronate affinity material can specifically bind to the compound containing cis-diol, thus enhancing the anti-interference ability of the fluorescence sensor. The boronate affinity effect refers to the reaction of boronic acid and cis-diol groups to form reversible cyclic esters, which is easy to achieve non-destructive binding to the target [18]. Consequently, boronate affinity materials are suitable for the identification and quantitative detection of compounds containing cis-diol groups [19]. The surface of QDs was modified and functionalized by boronate affinity material, and then the QDs with boronic acid groups were covalently bound to OTC with cis-diol groups to realize the selective detection of OTC in the environment. In addition, molecular imprinting is a molecular recognition technique that mimics the specific recognition of biomolecules such as enzymes and substrates, antigens and antibodies [20], and the molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) generated by this method can specifically recognize the target. The MIPs are formed by copolymerization of template molecules, functional monomers, and crosslinking agents under the action of initiators. After removing the template molecules, special binding sites complementary to the target are obtained in MIPs, which can achieve exclusive recognition of a specific target [21,22]. Due to good stability, specific recognition, and easy synthesis, MIPs are widely used in many fields such as photoelectrocatalysis, biomedicine, and optical detection [23–26]. Therefore, the combination of boronate affinity effect and molecular imprinting technology applied to the construction of non-toxic perovskite quantum dot materials is expected to further enhance the performance of perovskite quantum dot composite sensors, and it can achieve a double-effect improvement on the OTC detection effect.

In this work, a novel non-toxic PQDs fluorescent nanocomposite based on the combination of boronate affinity effect and molecular imprinting technology was designed to achieve highly selective detection of trace OTC. The non-toxic Cs3Bi2Br9 PQDs were prepared by the ligand-assisted reprecipitation method; the surface of Cs3Bi2Br9 PQDs was functionalized with boric acid; and Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs were prepared by the reverse microemulsion method. The optical properties and OTC detection ability of the Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs were investigated in detail. Meanwhile, the fluorescence spectrum of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs and absorption spectrum of OTC, fluorescence inhibition efficiency and fluorescence lifetime decay curves of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs were studied to verify the fluorescence detection mechanism. The use of non-toxic bismuth-based PQDs avoided the toxicity problem of lead element and achieved a green and pollution-free preparation process, and the combination of boronate affinity effect and molecular imprinting technology achieved high selectivity of the fluorescent nanocomposite. In addition, Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs can selectively detect OTC in real samples, expanding the application of environmentally friendly perovskite quantum dots in analytical detection.

Ethanol, acetone, dichloromethane, ammonia, n-hexane, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), oleylamine (OAm), and oleic acid (OA) were purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Triton X-100, 1-hexanol, cyclohexane, polyethylene glycol 200 (PEG), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethyl acetate (EAC), sulfamerazine (SMR), sulfamethoxazole (SMZ), o-phenylenediamine (OPD), BiBr3, CsBr, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), 3-aminophenyl boronic acid (APBA), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), 2,4,6-trichlorophenol (2,4,6-TCP), doxycycline (DC), and chlortetracycline (CTC) were all purchased from Aladdin Bio-chemistry Technology Co., Ltd. Sulfadiazine (SD) and acrylamide (AM) were obtained from Nanjing Dulai Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Toluene was purchased from Henan Haohua Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Oxytetracycline (OTC) was obtained from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. The chemicals and reagents used in the experiment were of analytical grade.

The morphologies of Cs3Bi2Br9 and Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs were observed using a Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). UV–visible absorption spectra were obtained by the Evolution 220 UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Thermo, USA). Fluorescence spectra were recorded on an EF97 XP spectrofluorophotometer (Shanghai, China). Fluorescence lifetime decay curves were performed with an Edinburgh FLS1000 transient steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). Infrared spectrum was collected on a Spectrum TWO Fourier infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and modification of Cs3Bi2Br9 PQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Cs3Bi2Br9 PQDs were prepared by the ligand-assisted reprecipitation method. In detail, 2.8 mL of DMF, 0.2 mL of DMSO, and 1.0 mL of PEG were mixed in a centrifuge tube, 0.06348 g of CsBr (0.3 mmol) was added and sonicated at 60℃ for 30 min to dissolve them completely. Then, 0.0897 g of BiBr3 (0.2 mmol) was added to 2.0 mL of EAC and the obtained solution was sonicated for 10 min to dissolve it. Subsequently, the above two solutions were mixed and 50 μL of OAm was added to form a light yellow precursor solution. At room temperature, 0.75 mL of the precursor solution was added to a rapidly stirred mixture of 5.0 mL of anhydrous ethanol and 5.5 mL of OA. After stirring for 1 min, the reaction solution was centrifuged for 5 min. Finally, the supernatant was retained for subsequent studies.

Cs3Bi2Br9 PQDs were modified with APBA to obtain boronic acid-functionalized Cs3Bi2Br9-APBA. Briefly, 0.00516 g of APBA was added to 10 mL of Cs3Bi2Br9 PQDs and stirred for 3 h at room temperature in the dark to obtain the Cs3Bi2Br9-APBA solution.

The highly selective and non-toxic Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs were successfully prepared using a reverse microemulsion method. Typically, 7.5 mL of toluene, 1.8 mL of 1-hexanol, and 1.8 mL of triton X-100 were added to a flask and stirred for 10 min. Then 3.0 mL of Cs3Bi2Br9-APBA solution was added to the above solution and stirred for 15 min to make a homogeneous mixture. Subsequently, 300 μL of TEOS and 800 μL of NH3-H2O were added and stirred thoroughly for 2 h, and then 80 μL of APTES and 20 mg of OTC were added and stirred for 24 h under light-avoidance conditions. After that, the generated solution was broken by adding 10 mL of acetone, and the precipitates were obtained by centrifugation. To remove the template molecules, the precipitates were washed using ethanol, then sonicated for 15 min and centrifuged again by repeating three times. Finally, the obtained imprinted polymers were dried at 60℃ in a vacuum for 12 h to obtain Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs. For comparison, the non-imprinted fluorescent nanocomposite (Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-NIPs) were prepared by the same process as the MIPs but without the addition of the template molecule OTC.

First, 4.0 mg of the dried imprinted polymer was dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol to obtain Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs ethanol solution, and then different volumes of OTC standard solution (1.0 mmol/L) were added. After a few minutes, the mixed solution was added to a fluorescence cuvette to measure the fluorescence value. The fluorescence detection of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-NIPs was carried out under the same procedure. The detection conditions of the instrument were as follows: excitation wavelength of 320 nm, slit width of excitation and emission wavelength of 10 nm, photomultiplier tube voltage of 750 V, and recorded emission wavelength range of 370 ∼ 450 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of OTC in soil samples
ce:section.ce:para: Soil samples from the bank of the Weihe River were collected as the actual samples, 1.0 g of dried and sieved soil was sonicated with 10 mL of ethanol for 30 min. The supernatant was retained after centrifugation, and the obtained precipitation was subjected to the above operation once again. The above two supernatants were mixed as a soil sample solution. Subsequently, 4.0 mg of dried imprinted polymer was added into different amounts of OTC solution colorimetric tubes, mixed with the soil sample solution to 10 mL, and fluorescence intensity was measured after the mixture was left to stand for 2 min.

The preparation of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs is shown in Fig. 1. The use of non-toxic bismuth instead of lead to form PQDs enables a green preparation process, and the specific recognition of OTC by boronic acid functionalization and molecular imprinting ensures the high selectivity of the Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs. The Cs3Bi2Br9 PQDs were prepared by the ligand-assisted reprecipitation method, the use of OA and OAm as ligands provided sufficient stability and ensured optical properties. The surface modification method was used to attach APBA to the surface of PQDs to form Cs3Bi2Br9-APBA, which enhanced the anti-interference ability of OTC detection. Finally, the Cs3Bi2Br9-APBA was combined with MIPs by the reverse microemulsion method to obtain Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs, which achieved accurate detection of OTC. The Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs were worked through van der Waals forces, hydrogen bonding, and boric acid covalent interaction with the template molecule OTC.

The morphologies of Cs3Bi2Br9 PQDs and Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs were investigated by transmission electron microscopy. As shown in Fig. 2a, the Cs3Bi2Br9 PQDs are uniformly distributed in a spherical shape with a particle size of about 5.0 nm. The Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs shown in Figs. 2b and 2c is also spherical and evenly distributed, with an increased particle size of 70 ∼ 100 nm. This is because the PQDs are encapsulated in a layer of silica, which results in a larger diameter of the nanospheres. The distribution of PQDs in the silicon spheres formed during molecular imprinting indicates the successful synthesis of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs.

The functional group’s structure of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs was analyzed by FT-IR as shown in Fig. 3. The absorption peaks at 1094.17 cm−1, 797.17 cm−1 and 465.94 cm−1 were attributed to the asymmetric stretching of Si-O-Si, symmetric stretching vibration and bending vibration of Si-O, respectively, which proved the synthesis of silicon spheres. The characteristic peak at 2926.93 cm−1 corresponded to the asymmetric stretching vibration of methylene (-CH2) of OA. The absorption peaks at 3410.30 cm−1 and 1634.69 cm−1 were the vibration of the amino group (-NH2) of APTES. These results indicated that the Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs were successfully prepared.

Fig. S1 shows the XRD image of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs. The observation shows that the sample still has Cs3Bi2Br9 (200) crystalline surface, which proves that the method successfully prepared Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs, and also indicates that the molecularly imprinted polymers have no effect on the structure of the PQDs. Fig. S2 shows the optimal emission and UV-absorption spectrum of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs. It can be seen from the figure that Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs has the maximum fluorescence emission peak at 400 nm under excitation at the wavelength of 320 nm.

In the preparation process of the Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs, the amount of TEOS and APTES has a very important effect on the optical properties and identification performance of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs. The amount of TEOS and APTES was determined by the fluorescence intensity and quenching efficiency of the Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs solution. 80 μL of APTES were used when determining the best use of TEOS. As shown in Fig. 4a, the fluorescence intensity of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs increased at the amount of TEOS, and the quenching efficiency was maximum when the amount of TEOS was 300 μL. So, 300 μL of TEOS were used when determining the best use of APTES. As shown in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence intensity of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs was the largest when the amount of APTES was 80 μL, and the quenching efficiency of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs was also high, so that the detection effect was remarkable. Therefore, 300 μL TEOS and 80 μL APTES were selected in the preparation process to obtain materials with good optical properties and identification performance.

To ensure the formation of specific identification cavities of the imprinted fluorescent nanocomposite, it was important to remove the template molecule OTC. The elution times of template molecules were determined by measuring the absorbance of OTC in detection solution at different number of ethanol solvent elution. The prepared Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs were dissolved in ethanol and sonicated for 10 min, the reaction solution was centrifuged to obtain the imprinted precipitates, and then the above operation was repeated. As shown in Fig. 5a, after two times of elution, the absorbance of the solution remained stable. To remove the template molecules as much as possible, the number of template molecules elution was determined to be 3 times.

In the detection of OTC, the concentration of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs solution affects the sensitivity and linear range of the fluorescence detection. The optimal detection concentration was determined by the relative relationship between fluorescence intensity and quenching efficiency. As shown in Fig. 5b, the trace amount of OTC could easily lead to fluorescence quenching when the concentration of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs was low, at this time, the nanocomposite was more sensitive to OTC detection, but the linear range was narrower. The fluorescence intensity of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs was higher at larger concentration, but the quenching efficiency was relatively small. To ensure the higher sensitivity and wider linear range of the fluorescent nanocomposite, 0.4 g/L was used as the optimal detection concentration of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs.

The pH value affects the fluorescence intensity and detection effect of the fluorescent nanocomposite. The optimal detection pH was determined by the fluorescence intensity of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs at different pH. As shown in Fig. 5c, the fluorescence intensity of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs gradually increased when the pH was in the range of 3.0 ∼ 7.0, and the fluorescence intensity reached its maximum at pH=7.0, indicating that the nanocomposite was greatly affected under acidic conditions. At pH＞7.0, the fluorescence intensity of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs slightly decreased with the increase in pH, indicating that the nanocomposite could maintain better fluorescence and detection performance in neutral and alkaline conditions. Therefore, the optimal detection pH for the subsequent experiments was 7.0, which was the pH value of anhydrous ethanol.

The stability of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs is closely related to the reliability and authenticity of detection results, which was assessed by fluorescence analysis. As shown in Fig. 6a, the fluorescence intensities of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs and Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-NIPs remained stable within 1 h, indicating that the fluorescent nanocomposite had good stability.

The optimal detection time is a key parameter for evaluating sensor performance. 5.0 μM OTC solution was added to the Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs solution, and the optimal detection time was determined by the fluorescence intensity corresponding to different times. As shown in Fig. 6b, the fluorescence intensity of the detection solution decreased rapidly and then basically stabilized at 2 min after the addition of the OTC solution, indicating that the Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs had a rapid response to OTC. Therefore, 2 min was selected as the detection response time for the subsequent experiments.

Under the optimal experimental conditions, the fluorescence detection abilities of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs toward different concentrations of OTC were evaluated. Different concentrations of OTC solutions were added to Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs and Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-NIPs solutions to record the corresponding fluorescence values. As shown in Fig. 7b, the best fluorescence emission wavelength was 400 nm, and the fluorescence intensity of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs at the best emission wavelength gradually decreased with the increase of OTC concentration from 0 to 130 μM. As shown in Fig. 7a, the linear relationship for F0/F of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs solutions and the OTC concentrations was F0/F=0.03034 COTC+1.01768 (R2=0.9975), and the linear range for detecting OTC concentrations was 0.8 ∼ 130 μM with the detection limit (3σ/k) of 0.32 μM.

The detection effect of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-NIPs on OTC was used as a comparison to demonstrate the quantitative detection of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs. As shown in Fig. 7d, the fluorescence intensity of the Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-NIPs decreased with the increase of the OTC concentration although the decreased degree was smaller than that of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs. As shown in Fig. 7c, the linear relationship was F0/F=0.02419 COTC+1.02614 (R2=0.9988), and the linear range for detecting OTC concentrations was 1.2 ∼ 70 μM. The comparison showed that Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs had higher sensitivity, better linear range, and linear quenching constant for OTC, which proved that Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs had better detection effect on OTC. By eluting the template molecule OTC during the molecularly imprinted binding process, cavity sites matching the size, shape, and function of the template molecule are left behind in the MIPs, thus obtaining the ability to specifically bind the template molecule and making the nanocomposite highly selective. Therefore, Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs have better detection ability for OTC. The imprinting factor (IF) is a general standard for evaluating molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensors. In this experiment, the IF of OTC detected by Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs was 1.254.

As shown in Table 1, Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs exhibits both better response time and LOD for OTC compared with other nanocomposites. The shorter response time is favorable for the practical application of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs, and the lower detection limit indicates the good sensitivity of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs. These results indicate that the prepared fluorescent nanocomposite has better fluorescence detection performance.

To evaluate the selectivity of the nanocomposite in OTC detection, several similar tetracyclines (TC, CTC, DC) were selected as other interfering substances. At room temperature, the fluorescence intensity of detection solution was measured by adding equal amounts of OTC and other similar tetracyclines to the Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs solution. As shown in Fig. 8a, it can be shown that the fluorescence quenching efficiency of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs for OTC was higher than other similar tetracyclines. As shown in Fig. 8b, when OTC was present along with other similar tetracyclines, interfering substances had little effect on OTC detection. The Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs were specific to OTC but nonspecific to other tetracyclines. The specific recognition of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs for OTC was attributed to the recognition sites on the surface of the Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs polymeric layer that specifically bound OTC, resulting in the fluorescence quenching.

Fluorescence quenching may be caused by several mechanisms, including static quenching, dynamic quenching, photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and inner filter effect (IFE) [33–36]. The mechanism of OTC quenching the fluorescence of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs was investigated using the systematic analysis method based on the main characteristics of the fluorescence quenching mechanism.

Firstly, it was verified whether the emission or excitation spectrum of the quantum dots overlapped with the absorption spectrum of the target. As shown in Fig. 9a, the UV–visible absorption spectrum of the OTC strongly overlapped with the emission spectrum of the Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs. Therefore, the possible mechanisms of fluorescence quenching were FRET or IFE. The probability of the IFE mechanism was validated to further study the mechanism. The fluorescence intensity corresponding to Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs in the presence of different concentrations of OTC was corrected according to Eq. (1).
(1)
F
corr
=
F
obs
× antilog
A
ex
+
A
em
2

Herein, Fobs is the observed fluorescence intensity, Fcorr is the corrected fluorescence intensity after subtracting the IFE quench fluorescence value from Fobs, Aex is the absorbance of OTC at the excitation wavelength of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs, and Aem is the absorbance of OTC at the emission wavelength of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs.

The inhibition efficiency E of corrected and observed fluorescence was calculated according to Eq. (2).
(2)
E =
1 −
F
F
0
× 100 %

The inhibition efficiency E of observed and corrected fluorescence of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs versus OTC concentration was shown in Fig. 9b, and it was inferred that IFE was the main mechanism of OTC quenching the fluorescence of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs, which accounted for about 81.14 %.

In addition, the fluorescence lifetime of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs was analyzed before and after OTC addition. The fluorescence lifetime of the quantum dots was measured using a transient fluorescence spectrometer to fit the fluorescence lifetime decay curves. One of the characteristics of the IFE mechanism is that the fluorescence lifetime of the sensor does not change before and after target addition, whereas FRET decreases the fluorescence lifetime. As shown in Fig. 9c, the fluorescence lifetime of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs hardly changed with the addition of OTC. The fluorescence lifetime curves could be fitted by the exponential decay curve (3).
(3)
I
t
=
I
0
+
A
1
exp
− t
τ
1
+
A
2
exp
− t
τ
2

The average fluorescence lifetime was fitted by the double exponential fitting model (4). Here, A is the amplitude of vibration, and τ is the fluorescence lifetime.
(4)
τ
ave
=
A
1
τ
1
2
+
A
2
τ
2
2
A
1
τ
1
+
A
2
τ
2

The transient fluorescence lifetime parameters obtained by fitting are shown in Table 3. R2 is the fit correlation, and the closer R2 is to 1 indicates that the fitted equation is more reliable and applicable. In the absence of OTC, the average fluorescence lifetime of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs was 1.57 ns. In the presence of OTC, the average fluorescence lifetime was 1.09 ns. The slight decrease in fluorescence lifetime suggested that the detection mechanism of the fluorescent nanocomposite could be a synergistic process of IFE and FRET.

To demonstrate the application potential of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs in real samples, Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs were used for OTC detection in soil samples. Since no OTC was found in the real sample, the standard addition recovery experiment was performed for fluorescence detection. The fluorescence intensity corresponding to different concentrations of OTC (0.8 ∼ 130 μM) was tested by Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs, and the concentrations of OTC were obtained using the fitting curve as shown in Table 4. It can be shown that the recovery was in the range of 93.33∼103.9 % and the relative standard deviation was 3.7∼7.9 %. These results suggested that Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs could be an effective method for OTC detection in real environmental samples.

In summary, an environmentally friendly perovskite quantum dot fluorescent nanocomposite (Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs) based on the combination of boronate affinity effect and molecular imprinting was used to achieve highly selective detection of OTC in ethanol. The use of the non-toxic bismuth element instead of lead element solved the toxicity problem of PQDs. The detection effect and response range of the fluorescent nanocomposite were effectively enhanced due to the specific recognition of OTC by the boronic acid groups and specific binding sites on the surface of Cs3Bi2Br9@SiO2-MIPs, the linear range of the concentration of OTC was 0.8 ∼ 130 μM with the detection limit of 0.32 μM. The fluorescent nanocomposite effectively solved the problems of poor selectivity and toxicity of PQDs in practical applications and had the characteristics of high selectivity, good sensitivity, and environmental friendliness. In addition, the nanocomposite was successfully applied to the trace detection of OTC in real environmental samples. This work provides a new strategy for the construction of environmentally friendly, highly selective, and high-performance PQDs fluorescent nanocomposite, which further expands the application of lead-free perovskite materials in the field of analytical detection.

Xiao Wei: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization. Yue Gao: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Data curation. Shen Zhang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Aixia Chen: Software, Methodology, Conceptualization. Jiali Liu: Investigation, Data curation. Fei Wang: Investigation, Data curation. Ilhem Kerboua: Investigation. Mengwei Yan: Data curation. Jiaqi Wang: Validation. Yuhui Zhang: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
